topic: Locating Africa on the World Stage

study_notes.title: Understanding the Regions and Capital Cities of Africa
study_notes.introduction: A capital city is the municipality holding primary status in a country, usually serving as the seat of the government. Africa is a vast continent divided into several major geographical regions, each containing countries with unique and historically significant capital cities.

East_Africa: Kenya - Nairobi (Started as a railway camp, known as the 'Green City in the Sun')
Tanzania - Dodoma (Official inland capital) / Dar es Salaam (Major coastal commercial port)
Rwanda - Kigali (Located in the 'Land of a Thousand Hills')
Burundi - Gitega (Recently moved from Bujumbura to centralize administration)
South Sudan - Juba (Located on the White Nile, capital of Africa's newest nation)
Horn_of_Africa: Djibouti - Djibouti (City shares the same name as the country)
North_Africa: Egypt - Cairo (Located near the River Nile delta)
Morocco - Rabat (Political capital, while Casablanca is the commercial center)
West_Africa: Nigeria - Abuja (Planned inland capital, moved from Lagos)
Ghana - Accra (Located exactly on the Prime Meridian)
Senegal - Dakar (The westernmost city on mainland Africa)
Mali - Bamako (Located on the River Niger)
Liberia - Monrovia (Named after US President James Monroe, settled by freed slaves)
Sierra Leone - Freetown (Known for its deep-water harbor, settled by freed slaves)
Central_Africa: Gabon - Libreville (Name means 'Free Town' in French)
Southern_Africa: South Africa - Pretoria (Administrative), Cape Town (Legislative), Bloemfontein (Judicial)
Island_Nations: São Tomé and Príncipe - São Tomé

unique_capital_facts: Kinshasa (DRC) and Brazzaville (Republic of Congo) are the closest capital cities in the world, sitting directly across the Congo River from each other.
South Africa is the only country in the world with three official capital cities.
Accra (Ghana) is geographically significant because the Prime Meridian (0° Longitude) passes directly through it.
Equatorial Guinea is unique because its capital, Malabo, is located on an offshore island (Bioko), not on its mainland territory.

examples: Nigeria: Moved from Lagos to Abuja.
Tanzania: Moved from Dar es Salaam to Dodoma.
Ivory Coast: Moved from Abidjan to Yamoussoukro.
Burundi: Moved from Bujumbura to Gitega.
reasons: To promote even economic development in the interior of the country.
To centralize government administration, making it accessible to all citizens.
To escape extreme overcrowding, traffic congestion, and poor sanitation in the old coastal cities.
To promote national unity by placing the capital in a neutral, central location.

capitals_hosting_international_organizat: Addis Ababa (Ethiopia) - Headquarters of the African Union (AU).
Arusha (Tanzania) - Headquarters of the East African Community (EAC).
Abuja (Nigeria) - Headquarters of the Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS).
